danger_signals.visual_form: Projectiles moving downward toward player sprite
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to player health; death occurs from being shot

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy sprite contact with player sprite
danger_signals.meaning: Direct damage or death; physical threat from outlaws and obstacles

danger_signals.visual_form: Player caught between obstacle and bottom of screen
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental trap; forced death condition if trapped

danger_signals.visual_form: Enhanced sprite with redesigned outfit (Bandit Bill) or shuriken symbol (Ninja)
danger_signals.meaning: Boss enemy encounter; heightened danger from named criminals

safety_signals.meaning: Extra protection layer; absorbs damage without death
Increased movement speed; improves evasion capability
Faster shot rate; increased offensive capability to clear threats faster
Longer shot range; extended reach to eliminate enemies before they approach
Extra life granted; safety buffer against death

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Bandit Bill (redesigned outfit in NES), Ninja (shuriken symbol added in NES), Cheyenne River variant, Death Mountain variant
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fort Wingate location; Cheyenne River location; Death Mountain location as stage settings

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Town of Hicksville mentioned in intro and ending narrative
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Hicksville referenced in stage progression narrative; town as conceptual safe haven (goal state)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Lone gunman appearing at sunset (narrative symbolism); bounty hunter archetype
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Player character sprite; three-button shooting mechanic (left/right/center) establishes player agency

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy prevalence in stages; Wingate Gang presence across Fort Wingate, Cheyenne River, Death Mountain locations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies actively attack player; continuous threat spawning throughout levels; gang members patrol territories

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player character moving upward through gang-controlled territories; defeated enemy sprites disappear; environmental hazards remain
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player shoots in three directions to clear path; collects upgrades and protective items; avoids environmental obstacles

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ending sequence displays narrative text confirming Wingate family destruction; Capcom logo or victory text replaces normal gameplay interface
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: No enemy spawning; stage progression halts; cutscene triggers with story message

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Narrative backdrop shown in intro text and story context; no visual representation in gameplay but establishes narrative urgency
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Motivates continuous wave of enemies; explains why Wingate Gang controls multiple distinct territories (Fort, River, Mountain)

environmental_storytelling.example: Fort Wingate, Cheyenne River, Death Mountain stages directly associate geographic locations with Wingate Gang control and danger escalation
NES version enhanced Bandit Bill's outfit and gave Ninja a shuriken symbol; portraits use sprite overlays for increased color, signaling importance of named criminals
Japanese version contextualizes story as Wingates murdering sheriff and taking control overnight; international versions emphasize lone gunman arriving at sunset—visual metaphor of hope at day's end

Three distinct boss music tracks (Boss Music 1, 2, 3) across three stage groupings signal escalating boss encounters; missing stage-specific tracks in FDS version due to hardware creates audio flattening of world state progression
Stars, bottles, bags, dragonflies as point-value items; horses, boots, bullets, rifles, yashichi as functional upgrades; visual distinction through sprite design communicates immediate utility

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Responds to three-button input (left/right/center shooting); can change shot direction via button combinations; collects items for upgrades; moves upward as screen autoscrolls
Must dodge enemy projectiles; must avoid collision with enemies and obstacles; can use collected protective items (horse) to survive hits
Spawn continuously in stage; move toward or fire upon player; occupy named locations (Fort, River, Mountain)

Named criminals (Bandit Bill, Ninja, Cheyenne River variant, Death Mountain variant, Fort Wingate boss, Wingate final boss) have distinct sprite designs; trigger special music; require concentrated fire to defeat
Autoscrolling upward; spawning waves of enemies; presenting obstacles; distributing collectible items; stage transitions trigger new music and enemy sets
Screen stops scrolling; enemy spawning ceases; ending text displays confirming Wingate family destroyed and town saved; narrative closure achieved

notes: Gun.Smoke uses primarily narrative and sprite-based visual communication. The NES version significantly enhanced faction visual identity through wanted poster redesigns compared to arcade version. The game's danger communication relies on continuous threat spawning and environmental hazard placement rather than color coding. Safety is communicated through discrete pickup items with distinct sprite designs. World state progression is tracked through stage location names (Fort Wingate, Cheyenne River, Death Mountain) that establish gang territory hierarchy. The shift from arcade (bounty hunter framing) to NES (town liberation narrative) added political context—the Wingates as occupying force and Billie Bob as liberator. Regional differences show Japanese version emphasized narrative context more heavily. Music variation (three boss tracks reused across stages due to FDS hardware limits) may have dampened world-state distinction in that version.
